name: Impetigo

key_questions: Number of lesions and extent of involvement?
Contagious setting: school/daycare, family members affected?
Underlying skin condition (eczema — common portal of entry)?
red_flags: Rapidly spreading or worsening despite treatment → MRSA, cellulitis
Bullous impetigo in infant — more serious (systemic S. aureus)
Post-strep glomerulonephritis risk (hematuria, edema) — with nephritogenic strains
Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome (SSSS): extensive blistering, fever, diffuse skin tenderness, positive Nikolsky sign — emergency referral
Necrotizing fasciitis: rapidly spreading erythema, disproportionate pain, systemic toxicity — emergency
ddx: Scabies (burrows, intense nocturnal itch, interdigital spaces)
Eczema herpeticum (atopic patient with punched-out erosions, fever)

first_line.name: Topical Antibiotic (localized)
first_line.drugs: Mupirocin 2% ointment (Bactroban) BID × 5 days | Fusidic acid 2% cream (Fucidin) TID × 5–7 days | Ozenoxacin 1% cream (Ozanex) BID × 5 days
first_line.notes: Topical therapy equivalent to oral for localized non-bullous impetigo. Reduces systemic antibiotic exposure and resistance risk. Pharmacist can prescribe under Ontario Minor Ailments (topical antibiotics only). SCOPE NOTE: Typically ≤3 localized lesions in one body area for topical-only MA management per OPA guidance. Clean crusts with warm water before applying. Mupirocin and fusidic acid cover S. aureus and Group A Strep. Ozenoxacin (Ozanex) 1% cream: Health Canada-approved 2017 for impetigo in patients ≥2 months; on Ontario Schedule 4 — a notable Canadian option for mupirocin-resistant cases.
first_line.preferred_when: Localized (≤3 lesions, one body area), non-bullous impetigo

second_line.name: Oral Antibiotic (widespread/bullous)
second_line.drugs: Cephalexin 500 mg QID × 7 days (children: 25–50 mg/kg/day ÷ QID) | Amoxicillin-clavulanate 500/125 mg TID × 7 days
second_line.notes: SCOPE NOTE: Oral antibiotics for widespread or bullous impetigo are OUTSIDE Ontario pharmacist Minor Ailments scope — refer to physician or NP. For widespread impetigo (>5 lesions), bullous type, or treatment failure with topical therapy. Cephalexin covers S. aureus (MSSA) and Strep. If MRSA suspected (no response to cephalexin): TMP-SMX DS BID × 7 days.

non_pharm: Keep lesions covered with gauze to prevent spread
School/daycare exclusion: child may return 24h after starting effective antibiotic treatment AND lesions are covered (Ontario PHU standard)
Do not share towels, washcloths, or bedding
Trim nails to prevent scratching and autoinoculation
refer_when: Widespread impetigo (>3 lesions or multiple body areas) — oral antibiotics required, outside MA scope
Bullous impetigo in any patient, especially infants
Failure to improve after 48–72h of topical antibiotic therapy
Facial impetigo near eyes or mucous membranes
Suspected staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome or necrotizing fasciitis — emergency

references: Ontario College of Pharmacists. Minor Ailments: Impetigo. OCP Prescribing Standards, O. Reg. 256/24 (2025).
AMMI Canada (Association of Medical Microbiology and Infectious Disease Canada). Skin and Soft Tissue Infection Guidelines (2019).
Stevens DL, et al. Practice Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Skin and Soft Tissue Infections: 2014 Update by IDSA. Clin Infect Dis. 2014;59(2):e10–e52.
NICE. Impetigo: Clinical Knowledge Summary (CKS). National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (2023).
